posts.name: Thierry Jutel
posts.cooked:
Hello Craig, I’m French and I tasted many 2015s in barrel in June 2016 (in between a few MTB rides, as you know).

They certainly tasted very ripe, and in my opinion, overripe in June 2016. And to be honest, most of the producers we visited (very reputable ones) certainly tended to think so to. I repeat the date (June 2016) because since, many critics and wine specialists and importers I trust have been telling us that the wines dramatically changed and gained structure and focus in the subsequent few months. Your experience, and despite the small sample, goes in the same direction.

I did buy some 15 reds (very few whites) and I will be looking at how this vintage develops in the future. If the wines turn out as good as serious critics and drinkers seem to say, I will tell the story of the barrel tasting to evoke the magic of Burgundy. If they turn out to be too soft and jammy, I’ll be able to tell I knew it all along.

Thierry

2015 is going to need time to shed some baby fat and come around. I believe that most wines will shut down pretty hard and need longer to come around. Say around 15 years for Borgogne and 17+ for Village,20+ for premier cru and Grand Crus could be misunderstood for a long long time. Yes I like the edgy nature of 2013 and I like the relaxed balance of 2014 and I like the sunshine in a bottle that is 2015, at certain times in the future each of these vintages will drink better than the others, but if I make it to my 75th birthday in 2046 I will be drinking 2015 Burgundy

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked:
To give another viewpoint to any fence sitters, Aubert de Villain described 2015 as a hypothetical blend of 2005 and 2003, and from what I’ve tasted so far I tend to agree.

If you like the idea of just a little fatter 2005, which many will, myself included, there is some of that out there and I’ve been trying to hunt some down. Had a gorgeous 2015 Nathalie Vigot Bourgogne the other night that was brilliant. But have also had a Leroy that tasted pruney and alcoholic, a pretty nice Hubert Lignier, and an overblown Morey Coffinet.

As a few of us pragmatists have pointed out, there are sure to be stellar wines this vintage, but if you like elevated acids along with concentrated fruit, a bit of dectective work should be amply rewarded, and is always fun.

posts.name: p@ulbortin

TN: 2015 Fourrier Gevrey Chambertin 1er Cru Cherbaudes VV: Pop & poured revealing reduction with fizz then decanted for an hour. Red fruit cherry and Gevrey underbrush nose. Palette is full bodied and fat with glycerin and similar flavors probably just bordering on the edge of not being overripe – but just barley.

TN: 2015 Fourrier Echezeaux VV: Pop and Poured. Minimal reduction. Terrific red fruit and Vosne Asian spice nose. Medium to full body but less concentrated than the Cherbaudes and probably better for it. Nice balance. A very nice Echezeaux that seems like a good bet at its price point.

TN: 2015 Hudelot Noellat Nuits st Georges 1er Murgers: Beautiful red and black cherry nose framed with Nuits sauvage notes. Full bodied and beautifully balanced red and black fruit flavors. 95pts. After tasting the superb 2010 version of this you have to wonder – is this the Domaine’s best wine?

Paul,
First, my apologies for the thread jacking. Second, I hope you are Martin’s new number 1 enemy as I am getting really tired of him chasing me around.

  • 2011 Domaine Armand Rousseau Père et Fils Chambertin - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Chambertin Grand Cru (11/7/2015)
    2015 Villa d’Este Wine Symposium - Kelly and Kevin’s excellent adventure; 11/3/2015-11/9/2015 (Villa D’este, Lake Como): Only a slight hint of the 04 like taint and much less so than the CDB. Decadent pure red and black fruits, blackberry, black cherry, strawberry, caramel, sap, mineral and earth. Very good concentration, harmonious and polished palate, good acidity, a hint of sweet tannins and medium finish. It is already quite delicious. A step up from the 11 CDB. (94 pts.)
  • 2011 Domaine Armand Rousseau Père et Fils Chambertin-Clos de Bèze - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Chambertin-Clos de Bèze Grand Cru (11/7/2015)
    2015 Villa d’Este Wine Symposium - Kelly and Kevin’s excellent adventure; 11/3/2015-11/9/2015 (Villa D’este, Lake Como): A hint of 11 taint and green, delicate black fruits, limestone and black pepper. Medium concentration, nicely layered, polished, decadent shy blackberry fruit, mineral, bright acidity and medium finish that ends with a hint of green. (92 pts.)
  • 2011 Domaine Armand Rousseau Père et Fils Chambertin-Clos de Bèze - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Chambertin-Clos de Bèze Grand Cru (9/11/2015)
    Rousseau Chambertin Clos de Beze event; 9/11/2015-9/12/2015 (Capella Hotel Georgetown): Oh no! Bill Nanson informed us unusually high concentration of lady bugs. So it really comes down to relative sensitive to the 04 like taint. I also posted my concerns regarding the 11. We both got grilled. This clearly shows the 04 like taint. It is not just unripe green. The first note is the 04 taint aka LBT then crushed blackberries, mineral and very earthy notes. Very good concentration, quite fresh, strong mineral presence, a hint of ash and metal that I associate with the 04 taint, good acidity and medium long finish. I recent drank the 04 version which I enjoyed despite a hint of taint. Caveat Emptor and YMMV. (92 pts.)

paul bortin:

Just off the top of my head, Gouges NSG Les St. Georges.

fred o.:

these personal attacks on Kevin

Fred, please identify one personal attack I have made on Kevin. He talks as if “the taint” is a given in 2011 red Burgundies. It’s not, and my calling him on it is not a personal attack. My opinion, formed from his many posts/notes on this board regarding almost exclusively high-end wines, is that he prefers rich, ripe wines, and that he is not likely to enjoy many 2011s. That, too, is not a personal attack.

fred o.:

For what it’s worth, the discussion on different vintages and possible taints has been helpful to newbies like me when I see others post their own tasting notes and opinions, rather than ad hominem swipes.

And this is part of the problem. Newbies like you being swayed by someone concluding that a vintage is “tainted” when there is a mountain of evidence that it isn’t. Do you know what “ad hominen” means? I have always challenged Kevin’s “taint” assertion and have not attacked him personally.

Alan Rath:

Rousseau poured his 11s at La Paulee in 2014. They were delicious and beautiful wines. I would drink them every day if I could afford them. Many other 11s were really beautiful as well, if a bit light and airy. BTW, not one of my notes from that tasting had the word “green” or “taint”.

This gets to my point. Alan, who has about as much knowledge and experience with Burgundy as anyone on this board, and whose preference runs to leaner, more elegant wines of transparency and finesse, does not find the 2011s “tainted.” And I don’t believe it is because he is less sensitive to “the taint.”

k s h i n:

I hope you are Martin’s new number 1 enemy as I am getting really tired of him chasing me around.

Kevin, you are hardly my enemy and I am not chasing you around - you post here a lot. And, when you start in on the 2011 “taint,” I am going to call you on it.

Yes, please, let’s discuss the 2015s.

posts.name: Martin Steinley

Craig G:

I just popped a 2015 Bertheau Chambolle 1er. I suppose it’s too small a sample size, but I would say anyone avoiding 2015 because it’s too ripe or extracted is nuts! This is lovely.

Craig, recently the 2015 Siguat Chambolle-Musigny Fuees showed beautifully, continuing to pick up steam over about three hours. It shows the richness and depth of the vintage, but nothing over the top. The 2015 Guillemot range is showing just how one would expect - some flesh on their otherwise willowy frames. Last week, the 2015 Vincent Santenay Beaurepaire Blanc was superb. A tad more ripeness and body seems to work well with what is otherwise a lean, focused laser.

Like Stan, given what I have seen in the 2015 Beaujolais crus (I continue to find wine after wine over the top ripe for my palate), I was somewhat apprehensive regarding 2015 red and white Burgundy, but my apprehension is waning as I taste more of them.

I come to the board to share my thoughts. I respect different opinions but I am really getting tired of Martin and his self righteousness.

I don’t know how long Martin has been drinking Burgundy but it has been over 30 years for me. I started working at my cousin’s wine shop just to learn English. I got curious as why people are spending over $20 for a bottle of wine when a Chavas or Jonnie Walker can be had for a lot less. The first bottle of wine that I fell in love was about ten year old bottle of the 76 Ponsot Morey St Demos Cuvee des Grives. Since then I got paid in wine.

I am not sorry that I am blessed to drink the “trophy” burgundies. I see the beauty in clean and pure Bourgogne blanc but I love the complexity and finesse in the top 1er and GC reds more. As far as I know, this is not a crime.

posts.name: Max Kelly

I really don’t want to add fuel to this fire, but in my far more limited experience, arguments over vintages for red or white Burgundy en masse seems a bit fruitless (no pun intended).

We don’t drink vintages, we drink wines. There is so much variation between and within vineyards (combes versus hotter areas, location on the slope, or distribution of holdings within a vineyard), and how vignerons manage vintage conditions in the field and cellar, that extrapolating directly from the vintage to individual wines doesn’t make a lot of sense to me. I’m thinking of 2007s here, for example.

I look forward to being sharply and emphatically corrected. .gif

This is kind of a bizarre take on Martin’s message.

I don’t agree with Martin on everything he says, but taking a disagreement to PM is a conventional, frequently-advocated, step when the discussion between two individual starts to take up a lot of space and threatens to become personal. It’s a measure of discretion and courtesy towards the board members. Construing it as an effort to ‘silence’ Kevin, imo, is a mistake and, really, unfair.

On the other hand, so long as the discussion stops short of personal attacks, a heated debate can be a really good way to get different ideas out into the open. Speaking for myself, the Shin-Martin exchange in this thread has provided some interesting food for thought. Thanks to both: you’re both evidently thoughtful and committed to your views.

Some people like to buy wines by category and vintage; for others, half the fun of drinking Burgundy is knowing the details of each producer and vineyard, year-to-year. There’s room for both approaches.

If you adopt a perspective sympathetic with individual producers, in any event, you might reasonably take exception to broad-brush, vintage-wide characterizations that may unfairly ‘taint’ the fortunes and reputations of vignerons who have managed their properties skillfully in difficult circumstances. Those more focussed on buyers’ fortunes might be especially on-guard against possible hype and efforts to gloss over identifiable, systematic flaws (extreme case: premature oxidation). Forum members are fortunate to benefit from the exchange of different points of view, as well as information from a variety of sources.

posts.cooked: Kevin doth protest too much. Quite simply, I have refuted his assessment that the entire 2011 red Burgundy vintage has “the taint” (whatever that is). Since doing so, I have noted his propensity to rave about about big, excessively ripe wines. See for example here TN: Comprehensive 2000 St Emilion Blind Tasting - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers and just recently here TN: 2009 Paul Jaboulet Aîné Hermitage La Chapelle - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers. Thus, I suggested that the moderately concentrated, low alcohol 2011s might be contrary to Kevin’s preferences. Kevin has construed that as me questioning his palate and as a personal attack. Kevin now mentions that the 2011s lack purity and energy and that pricing was too high upon release. Those are entirely different assessments than the vintage being tainted, i.e., contaminated or polluted, which I believe is a reckless assessment. As stated above, the 2011s were too expensive upon release and they are only moderately concentrated (they definitely don’t have “incredible concentration,” of which Kevin says above he is a “big fan”). In sum, I disagree with Kevin’s blanket “taint” assessment, which I have postulated being attributable to him preferring bigger, riper wines. If that is a personal attack, I am guilty as charged.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: Speaking of another look, I had the 2015 Guillemot Savigny-Les-Beaune VV last night. It may be the most darkly-colored Guillemot wine I have had and it definitely is riper and more concentrated than usual for Guillemot, but it has plenty of old-vine sap and drive. It is a $35 wine that I expect will look pretty smart in 15 years, and the 1er crus even better. Tonight, the 2015 Lucien Boillot (son Pierre, brother-in-law of Ghislaine Barthod, now in charge) Pommard 1er cru Les Croix Noires is loaded with the freshness and vigor that can make drinking young wines a treat. It is darkly-colored and the aromas are of dark fruit. On the palate, it is perfectly ripe and not more, with a core of red and black fruit, and it is broad and long. The silky texture is that of old vines (about 80 years-old). I believe that the larger frame of this wine carries the ripeness of 2015 better than that of the village Guillemot. This wine and the domaine’s Gevrey-Chambertin Cherbaudes (95 year-old vines) are gems for under $100.

About young vintages in Burgundy

We have more and more Producers who are aware about the fact that many amateurs are drinking young vintages instead of keeping them for at least 5 to 10 years.

For this reason, they have done slight and smooth changes in their vinification process in order to give to their young wines some friendly appreciation.

Of course, for the grands crus, they still ask to stay calm and wait and some names like Coche-Dury for his Corton-Charlemagne or Frédéric Mugnier for his Musigny have decided to put them on the markets at a future date.

This year, At our event at Villa d’Este, Eric Rousseau has decided, in this respect, to put for his Chambertin and St Jacques those vintages : 2012 – 2010 – 2009 – 2006.

And Aubert de Villaine will present at the Impero room these 3 crus :
Romanée-St-Vivant 2014 ; Echezeaux 2009; Bâtard Montrachet 2008.

The last vintages in Côte de Nuits, 2015, 2016 and probably the 2017 will need also to be kept at least 5 to 10 years and I think, in this respect, that more and more top names will wait a little more in order to put them on the markets.

BTW : it seems that Clos de Tart will be sold : Burgundy is really under the big research of investors who do not care too much about quick return but more as a sauvegarde of their wealth for future generation.

Burgundy has a level of love from amateurs that is clearly putting this region at the very top of the best in the world.

Fred C:

Jeremy mentioned that winemakers are learning to extract less and work with warmer vintages to prevent the overripe issue. He says he feels the 2015 is closer to 2005 but with less harsh tannins. A wine to age.

That tracks with the impressions of various winemakers as related by Doug Barzelay last December. From his blog:

DRC made brilliant wines in 2015, the best of which may ultimately stand comparison with ’99 and ’90. (Aubert de Villaine, though, favors a comparison of ’15 to a mix of ’03 and ’05–the richness of ’03 but without the surmaturité, and the structure of ’05 without the strong tannins.



Sébastian Cathiard…Discussing the inevitable comparison with 2005, he noted that the 2015s had silkier tannins.



Christophe said they have a similar balance to the ‘05s, but that the tannins in ’05 were harder, and that waiting to pick in ’15 seems to have paid off.



The affable, talented Jean-Marie Fourrier compared ’15 to a cross between ’09 and ’10, but said that overall, what really sets this vintage apart is the texture of the wines. He noted that it was a vintage characterized by small berries, and an exceptional ratio of skins to juice, and that the challenge in ’15 was to keep balance and purity, and not to over-extract.



Paul Zinetti [at Comte Armand] compared 2015 to 2010 (the other one who mentioned ’10 as a comparative was Jean-Marie Fourrier), citing their balance and elegance.

Even if it was a perfect growing season, not all need to doing back flips about the wines. Call it “some kind of wine elite” or contrarian view, but based upon what I have tasted thus far, 2015 red Burgundy is ripe. Anyone cellaring red Burgundy should, IMO, acquire some 2015s, but unless they are someone who shares Nick’s unequivocal preference for ripe Burgundy, I don’t believe that they should fill their cellar. (And, by the way, most red Burgundy that I am offering right now is 2015.)

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Jasper Morris wrote in his Inside Burgundy – 2010 - page 15 at the end of the paragraph Understanding burgundy:

((((Among the beautiful things in my life have been some wonderful bottles of burgundy. Not necessarily grands crus, not always great vintages – but wines which, from the first sniff, have demonstrated that the vigneron has done the best possible job with the grapes available from that vineyard, in that year.

Burgundy does not respond well to being put in the straitjacket. There are no set rules to making burgundy; there are no set rules to appreciating burgundy. It intrigues, fascinates delights, infuriates, disappoints, charms, enraptures and puzzles. Very like the life of a man, as long as it refrains from Hobbes’ definition – ’solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short’. This book aims at lighting the way towards bottles which are rick in flavor, delightful, civilized and long – and certainly plural.))))

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: Jean-Marie Fourrier describes 2015 as a cross between 2009 and 2010. In the Jasper Morris piece which Max provided above, Morris states that at some point in their evolution the 2015s may come across as “alarmingly rich and ripe,” but will eventually come together. For my palate, the 2015 Fourrier Gevrey-Chambertin Aux Echezeaux is more 2009 than 2010 and, although perhaps not alarmingly so, it is definitely ripe. The nose is of sweet red fruits, cranberry-like, and portends what’s to come. The attack, too, is (fruit) sweet and masks any nuance at this very early stage. The acids are good, so the wine never becomes heavy. Also, as it opens, it tightens some and gains focus. Indeed, there is potential here, but I expect that this will always be for those who like riper wines than I do.

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked:
Just drank 2 very different Gevrey Chambertin VV…


2015 Domaine Denis Bachelet:This is an incredible Village level wine that will compete with many a great Premier Cru. Gevrey and Bachelet markers of tart, dark cherries, iron, game blood, dusty iron/clay mineral, it is easy going, but with great depth. This is shiny and excellent, with power just below the surface. Man is this great! This will reward today, say if you drank it slowly over an evening…

2015 Domaine Fourrier GCVV: Disappointing. What a letdown after an amazing Bachelet GCVV ffrom the same vintage. This comes out clunky and out of balance. The fruit is quite ripe perceptually, almost candied, ugh. There is some spice, and pine box. Finish resolves with something missing? Revisit with caution.

I want to definitely go for some more Bachelet. I would love for someone to tell me the Fourrier was an anomaly as I have been buying for years.

posts.name: craig v
posts.cooked: I started getting serious about Burgundy with the 2002 vintage. 2002 was heralded as an amazing vintage. To date without backtracking into much older vintages 2010 is the best vintage I’ve run across. I was hoping 2015 might be another 2010. That said, at 62yrs old I’m constraining myself to bourgogne and village level wines while I keep my eyes out for remaining good value from 2005,2006,2007,2008 and 2010.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Charlie Carnes:

I would love for someone to tell me the Fourrier was an anomaly as I have been buying for years.

Charlie, you are likely going to get a lecture about not being able to assess these wines at this early stage, but your experience with these two wines is consistent with my experience with the vintage as a whole (i.e. a superb wine, followed by one I find too ripe). No doubt, for those who prefer decidedly ripe wines, they can probably throw a dart at the 2015s and get a wine they like or love, but for those who prefer Burgundy for its cool freshness, precision and detail, and weightless intensity, I do not believe that it is a back-up-the-truck vintage. That said, I expect that some wines will stretch out with time in the cellar and likely serve well with appropriate food at the dinner table (but they will always be ripe).

YacobovE:

I also spent last night reading this thread from start to finish and I’m still confused on where people are shaking out! With '15s available in bottle for the last ~6 months and the Paulee '15 Grand Tasting behind us, do folks have additional or revised thoughts on the vintage?

A moderately substantial report on 2015s from bottle is forthcoming in The Wine Advocate. I’m writing up notes at the moment. I won’t get into particulars before they’re published, but suffice it to say that I think folks that have purchased 2015 reds judiciously will be very happy. There are some magical wines. But, as Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier puts it, this is a vintage made for people with cellars, and the wines will be better at age twenty than they are at age ten.

There have been a few wines—and before this comment runs away with itself, we’re really talking about a handful—which show a bit stewy and overripe from bottle, despite having been quite seductive from barrel, so working through many of them again has been instructive. But one is more likely to be surprised at how bright the acids are despite the evident ripeness of the fruit.

Happy to respond to questions in so far as I’m able.

posts.cooked:
William Kelley:
YacobovE:

I also spent last night reading this thread from start to finish and I’m still confused on where people are shaking out! With '15s available in bottle for the last ~6 months and the Paulee '15 Grand Tasting behind us, do folks have additional or revised thoughts on the vintage?

A moderately substantial report on 2015s from bottle is forthcoming in The Wine Advocate. I’m writing up notes at the moment. I won’t get into particulars before they’re published, but suffice it to say that I think folks that have purchased 2015 reds judiciously will be very happy. There are some magical wines. But, as Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier puts it, this is a vintage made for people with cellars, and the wines will be better at age twenty than they are at age ten.

There have been a few wines—and before this comment runs away with itself, we’re really talking about a handful—which show a bit stewy and overripe from bottle, despite having been quite seductive from barrel, so working through many of them again has been instructive. But one is more likely to be surprised at how bright the acids are despite the evident ripeness of the fruit.

Happy to respond to questions in so far as I’m able.

Do you feel that twenty-year rule will apply for “lesser” wines too (high-quality lieu-dits, mid-low level premier crus), or just on the high-end premier crus and grand crus?

I also spent last night reading this thread from start to finish and I’m still confused on where people are shaking out! With '15s available in bottle for the last ~6 months and the Paulee '15 Grand Tasting behind us, do folks have additional or revised thoughts on the vintage?

A moderately substantial report on 2015s from bottle is forthcoming in The Wine Advocate. I’m writing up notes at the moment. I won’t get into particulars before they’re published, but suffice it to say that I think folks that have purchased 2015 reds judiciously will be very happy. There are some magical wines. But, as Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier puts it, this is a vintage made for people with cellars, and the wines will be better at age twenty than they are at age ten.

There have been a few wines—and before this comment runs away with itself, we’re really talking about a handful—which show a bit stewy and overripe from bottle, despite having been quite seductive from barrel, so working through many of them again has been instructive. But one is more likely to be surprised at how bright the acids are despite the evident ripeness of the fruit.

Happy to respond to questions in so far as I’m able.

Do you feel that twenty-year rule will apply for “lesser” wines too (high-quality lieu-dits, mid-low level premier crus), or just on the high-end premier crus and grand crus?

In short, no—adding that producer style will be just as important, as e.g. the Georges Noëllat 2015s will drink much earlier than e.g. Mugneret-Gibourg’s, irrespective of appellation.

But I should add the caveat that we haven’t yet seen the full extent of how much the 2015s will shut down. There is an argument that the '15s won’t shut down as hard as the '05s, as the quality of the phenolic material is superior. But on the other hand, it’s more abundant. There is just so much substance there that it’s hard not to imagine that it will assert itself at some point.

Happily, we have what looks to be a superabundance of supple, open-knit and succulent 2017s waiting in the wings - they’ve already mostly completed malolactic fermentation - that seem set to be delicious in their youth.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
I’ve been working through a few 2015s this week as I received an unnaturally large shipment from Martin (I had to confess three years worth of sins).

Domaine de la Cadette Bourgogne Vézelay la Châtelaine (Blanc) was very tasty but a bit of what I originally expected from 2015 whites: Fairly ripe and lacking a bit of acid.

Domaine Jean Marc Vincent Auxey Duresses Les Hautés (Blanc) was much better. It’s clearly from a ripe vintage but has fine acidity and minerality. Very good.

Frédéric Esmonin Gevrey Chambertin Les Jouises is beautiful. It has excellent richness and complexity as well as impeccable balance, and a streak of crushed rock that is pure Burgundy. There’s quite a bit of lurking tannin so I wouldn’t be surprised if it shuts down, but as of now it drinks perfectly.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Agreed, Jean-Marc Vincent did a great job with his '15 whites. They were almost electric from barrel.

From bottle, his 2014 Santenays are electric, and his 2015s maybe better because they are almost electric, as you say. Craig, from my experience, his 2015 Santenay blanc cuvees are a step above the Auxey-Duresses.

Here is what Jeremy H. said about a couple of Vincent’s whites during his 2017 romp through France, which I pulled from his thread here on that topic:

2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Auxey-Duresses Les Hautes: Already expressive with notes of citrus blossom, white peach and preserved lemon. There’s good richness and a strong line of minerally acidity.

2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay 1er Cru Beaurepaire Blanc: Tight and dense. There are yellow fruits and plenty of saline minerality. It has great shape and line and is so fresh and direct.

Disclaimer: I sell Jean-Marc Vincent’s wines.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Back to Fourrier - having tasted through a few of these ‘15s at La Paulee I have to disagree on all this hating. I found these wines on the riper side for red Burgundy, but in no way overly ripe. Generally they seem to me to be a wonderful combination of density and richness paired to lightness and balance. Clear red fruit that increases in intensity as you go up the scale from village to CSJ. I’m a fan.

And as someone who drinks a goodly amount of California Pinot, these Fourriers (and indeed the rest of the ‘15s I’ve tasted) ain’t nothing like California. Fourrier or otherwise, I didn’t taste anything from Burgundy that was close in terms of the kinds of sweetness, richness and darker aromatic notes that I see in the Cali Pinots I like (and my taste doesn’t stretch to Russian River cola bombs).

Btw in terms of QPRs I was really impressed by 2015 Volnays at La Paulee. Amazing fresh vibrant floral noses on many of these, they are going to be great. Eg. Rossignol, Clerget and Pousse d’Or.
